headlines.geopolitics: Iranian Military Shows It Knows How to Adapt, U.S. Officials Say
headlines.economy: Culture at Columbia Shielded Sexual Assault by Physician, Report Finds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll: Voters expect AI to transform our lives — but today is not that day

metadata.daily_do: Check one piece of content today before sharing it — ask yourself 'could this be AI-generated?' and look for the tells.

clips.caption: When Duke accidentally becomes the AI whisperer 😂
Dr. Pringle destroys the 'AI is like the internet' comparison
Palantir using AI to find office chairs is peak 2026

script.text: Everyone keeps saying artificial intelligence is like the internet in the 1990s. It is not. And the reason why should terrify you.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction for Monday, March 10th. Let's start with what's happening in the world.
First, geopolitics. Iran's military is showing tactical adaptation according to U.S. officials, specifically targeting what they see as American vulnerabilities in regional air defense systems.
Of course they are.

Second, domestic institutions. An external investigation found Columbia University and NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital mishandled sexual assault allegations against physician Robert Hadden. Two affiliated doctors have resigned.
Institutional protection again. Classic.

And third, technology and society. A new Fox News poll shows 87% of voters expect AI to transform American life, but only 47% find it useful right now. Meanwhile, 80% are concerned about AI eroding trust in news and social media.
So basically we're all waiting for the robot apocalypse but can't get ChatGPT to write a decent grocery list.
That's... actually not wrong.
Also in that same poll, 74% of voters believe extraterrestrial life exists.
More people believe in aliens than find AI useful. That tracks.
Given the vastness of the universe, the alien statistic is actually quite reasonable.
Doc just casually defending his space friends.
Let's dig into this AI polling data because it reveals something interesting about where we are right now. Bree, you've been tracking tech policy — what's your read on this disconnect?
It's actually fascinating, um, because what you're seeing is this massive expectation gap. People know something big is coming but they can't feel it yet in their daily lives.
And that 80% concern about AI eroding trust? That's not hypothetical anxiety. We're already seeing deepfakes in political campaigns, AI-generated news articles, synthetic voices.
But here's what kills me about this poll. The people who find AI most useful? Men under 45 and Republicans under 45. You know what that tells me?
That it's early adopter bias?

That the people with the most free time to mess around with new technology are the ones who like it. Meanwhile, women without college degrees, rural voters, people making under fifty grand — they're not impressed.
Because they need technology that actually solves real problems, not toys.
That's actually a really good point. But the trust erosion issue — that's hitting everyone equally. Rich, poor, educated, not educated. Everyone's worried about not being able to tell what's real.
The poll shows 60% think they can tell if something is AI-generated. That number hasn't changed since last June.
Sixty percent of people think they can spot AI content. I guarantee you sixty percent of people cannot spot AI content.
My neighbor posted a picture of his cat last week that looked weird to me. I spent twenty minutes trying to figure out if it was AI. It was just a bad photo.
Ha! But that's exactly the problem. We're becoming paranoid about everything because we can't trust our own perception anymore.
So what's the policy response here? The poll shows 89% want clear disclosure when content is AI-generated. That's about as close to universal agreement as you get on anything.
Right, and we're starting to see platforms respond. X just announced they're requiring disclosure for AI-generated conflict videos. But enforcement is the real question.
Good luck enforcing that. You're gonna have a twenty-two-year-old content moderator in Dublin trying to figure out if a video from Syria is real or fake.
Which is why we need— look, this is where regulation actually makes sense. Technical standards, watermarking requirements, things that are built into the technology itself.
Until someone builds an AI that removes the watermarks.
Then you build better watermarks! This is an arms race, not a surrender.
The poll also shows that 52% are concerned AI will eventually take control of humans. Duke, as someone who's not particularly worried about robot overlords, what's your take?
My take is that we can't get AI to reliably book a restaurant reservation, but we're worried it's gonna enslave humanity. Let's focus on the immediate problems.
Wait, you guys are using AI to book restaurant reservations?
No, that's my point. It can't do it.
Oh. I was about to say, that sounds useful.
But the existential risk concern isn't crazy. When you have 44% of people extremely concerned about AI eroding trust in media, that's a democracy problem.
Interesting demographic split there too. Very conservative voters and MAGA supporters are most concerned about AI taking control of humans. That's not what I would have predicted.
Makes sense to me. If you don't trust institutions, you're not gonna trust the institutions building the AI.
Which is actually a reasonable concern! These are the same tech companies that couldn't protect our data, and now they're building artificial minds.
So we have this interesting moment where the technology isn't quite here yet, but the social anxiety about it absolutely is.
Professor, everyone's comparing AI adoption to the early internet. Is that the right historical parallel?
Everyone keeps saying artificial intelligence is like the internet in the 1990s. It is not. And the reason why should terrify you.
Okay, now I'm nervous.
The internet in the 1990s was a communication tool that people could understand. You sent an email. You visited a website. You could see what it was doing.
Yes, there was skepticism — I remember Newsweek's famous 1995 article predicting the internet would fail. But the skepticism was about adoption, not about fundamental reality.

Artificial intelligence is different. It's not a tool we use — it's a mirror of human cognition that we cannot fully understand or control. When someone creates content with AI, they often don't know how it was made.
Huh. I never thought about it that way.
The better parallel is the printing press in 1450. Johannes Gutenberg didn't just create a new communication tool — he created a technology that could replicate human knowledge at scale.

And what happened? Within fifty years, you had the Protestant Reformation, political upheaval across Europe, the end of the medieval information monopoly. The printing press didn't just change how we communicated — it changed who had the power to shape reality.
So you're saying AI is gonna cause religious wars?
I'm saying AI is going to change who controls the construction of truth. And historically, when that changes, everything else changes too.
Oh god. That's why 80% of people are worried about trust in media. It's not just about fake videos — it's about the entire information ecosystem.
Precisely. The internet gave everyone a printing press. AI gives everyone the ability to create content that appears to come from human intelligence — including intelligence they don't possess.
So when I asked ChatGPT to explain quantum physics and it gave me an answer that sounded smart, I had no way to verify if it was actually correct.
Exactly. And neither did the AI. It was producing the statistical pattern of what a correct explanation should look like.
That's a genuinely unsettling way to think about this technology.
The printing press took 200 years to fully transform European society. AI is moving faster. Much faster.
Alright, let's shift gears. Jax, what else is happening in the world that we should know about?
Well, speaking of AI, Palantir — the company that builds surveillance systems for the government — has deployed artificial intelligence to help find office chairs for government staff.
I'm sorry, what?
They used their fancy AI weaponry to optimize furniture procurement. We've gone from 'we need AI to fight terrorism' to 'we need AI to find ergonomic seating solutions.'
That's actually peak government efficiency right there.
In my day, you found office chairs by walking to the supply closet.
Moving on. Louisiana classrooms are required to post the Ten Commandments by this Friday. Because apparently the state legislature decided Moses had some thoughts on curriculum standards.
I mean, 'thou shalt not cheat' probably applies to homework.
I can't tell if you're joking.
Neither can I.
In Florida news — and yes, it's always Florida news — a drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt.
Months?
Months. Like, this wasn't a weekend timeout. This was a whole seasonal imprisonment situation.
How did nobody notice?
It's Florida. A man screaming from a storage unit probably didn't even make the top ten weird sounds in the neighborhood.
That's dark, man.
And finally, in Texas, someone named 'Literally Anybody Else' is on the mayoral ballot. Which honestly might be the most honest political campaign slogan I've ever seen.
Wait, that's their actual legal name?
Apparently they legally changed it just to run for office. Which is either the greatest political stunt ever or a cry for help from Texas voters.
Honestly? They might win.
'Literally Anybody Else' versus 'Generic Incumbent.' That's the choice America deserves.
In ancient Athens, they sometimes selected officials by lottery. Perhaps we're coming full circle.
Doc just endorsed random candidate selection. 2026 is wild.
Alright, let's wrap with the Daily Do. Given everything we talked about today around AI and trust in information, here's something concrete you can do.
Before you share any piece of content today — a news article, a video, a social media post — ask yourself one question: 'Could this be AI-generated?' Then look for the tells.

Weird shadows in photos, text that's too perfect, videos where the lip sync looks off. It's not about becoming paranoid — it's about training your eye to notice when something feels constructed rather than authentic.
And if you can't tell, that's valuable information too.
Exactly. The goal isn't to be right every time. It's to be intentional about what you're consuming and sharing.
As Aristotle said, 'It is the mark of an educated mind to be able to entertain a thought without accepting it.'
I'm Jax Mooney. Thanks for hanging out with us. We'll be back tomorrow to figure out what the hell happened overnight.
